progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is well motivated and clearly written, with a compelling central idea: reframing entity retrieval as constrained autoregressive generation of canonical entity names rather than classification over atomic entity IDs. The introduction does a strong job identifying practical limitations of dense bi-encoder retrieval—memory footprint, negative sampling, limited interaction modeling—and the examples in Figure 1 effectively illustrate why entity names can provide useful compositional structure. The main contribution appears original and significant, especially if the reported broad empirical gains and memory reductions hold across the many claimed tasks. That said, the framing risks overstating some advantages: generating names still requires constrained decoding over a large entity vocabulary/trie, so latency and scalability should be examined as carefully as memory; “exact softmax” is exact over next-token vocabularies rather than over entities, which may introduce length and surface-form biases; and the method’s dependence on unique, stable, unambiguous names may be a limitation for KBs without Wikipedia-like titles or for entities with many aliases. The claim that the model “cross-encodes” context and entity is also somewhat different from cross-encoding candidate descriptions, since candidate-specific evidence is represented only through the generated string unless additional metadata is used. The most important issues for the full paper to address are therefore rigorous efficiency comparisons, robustness to ambiguous/rare/new entities and aliases, calibration/ranking behavior under constrained beam search, and whether gains come from genuine retrieval ability versus memorized factual/name priors in the pretrained sequence-to-sequence model. Overall, the paper appears strong, readable, and potentially impactful, but its practical trade-offs and assumptions about entity naming need careful empirical validation.

comments.title: Memory-scaling claims conflate learned parameters with candidate-set/trie storage
comments.quote: (ii) the memory footprint is greatly reduced because the parameters of our encoder-decoder architecture scale with vocabulary size, not entity count; / Also, the memory footprint required is orders of magnitude smaller than current systems, since the parameters of a sequence-to-sequence model scale linearly with the vocabulary size, not entity count.
comments.explanation: The core advantage is valid: GENRE does not need a learned output weight or dense vector per entity, so its neural parameters are not linearly tied to the number of entities. However, the total retrieval system still needs to store the valid entity identifiers for constrained decoding, typically as a trie, and the paper later reports a Wikipedia-title trie occupying substantial memory. The claim should distinguish learned-parameter memory from candidate-set/index memory, which still scales with the entity inventory.

comments.title: “Exact softmax” should be qualified as token-level rather than entity-level normalization
comments.quote: the ability to compute an exact softmax without the need to subsample negative data
comments.explanation: GENRE avoids negative entity sampling by training a sequence generator with an exact decoder softmax over the token vocabulary at each step. In an entity-retrieval context, however, “exact softmax” can be read as an exact softmax over all entities or valid candidate names. GENRE does not compute such an entity-level normalizer during training; it changes the objective to title-sequence likelihood, where non-gold entities are contrasted only indirectly through next-token choices. The claim is therefore correct for the decoder vocabulary softmax but should not imply exact entity-level classification likelihood.

comments.title: Name-only cold-start claims are stronger than the demonstrated capability
comments.quote: Finally, we demonstrate that new entities can be added by simply specifying their unambiguous name. / This demonstrates the ability of our solution to be applied in scenarios where entity metadata is unavailable (apart his name), a setting where, to the best of our knowledge, no existing system is capable to operate.
comments.explanation: Adding a new title to the constrained decoding set does make that entity decodable without retraining an entity classifier or learning a new embedding. But inserting a name does not by itself supply aliases, descriptions, factual knowledge, or associations needed to retrieve the entity from arbitrary contexts. The broader claim that no existing system can operate with only entity names is also not established without title-only lexical or dense baselines. The evidence supports the narrower claim that GENRE can add name-only candidates to its output space, not that reliable retrieval or uniqueness over prior methods is guaranteed.

comments.title: Constrained decoding descriptions leave terminal and state-transition details implicit
comments.quote: For each node _t ∈T_ , its children indicate all the allowed continuations from the prefix defined traversing the trie from the root to _t_ . / Finally, when generating an entity, the decoder employs the entities trie such that it can only output a valid entity identifier as in Constrained Beam Search explained above.
comments.explanation: The trie-based constrained decoding idea is clear, but the description omits several boundary conditions needed for exact reproducibility. A valid entity name can be a prefix of a longer entity name, so the trie must represent terminal states or EOS/closing-delimiter options explicitly. In the dynamic markup setting, the decoder must also maintain an input-position pointer, markup state, and entity-trie state; specify when EOS is allowed; prevent or define empty mentions; and state how closing delimiters interact with valid entity prefixes. These are likely straightforward implementation choices, but they should be made explicit.

comments.title: Calling the predefined entity set “relatively small” obscures the real tractability distinction
comments.quote: Differently from a setting where the output space is relatively small (e.g., a pre-defined set _E_ ), the space of annotated outputs is exponentially large.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast is valid: a fixed entity set can be represented by a precomputed trie, whereas full annotated outputs combine many possible span segmentations and entity choices. However, describing the predefined entity set as “relatively small” is misleading in a paper that emphasizes millions of Wikipedia entities. The distinction is not that _E_ is small, but that it is fixed, finite, and compactly representable relative to the exponentially large structured markup space.

comments.title: Table 2 caption understates the number of out-of-domain EL datasets
comments.quote: Table 2: Micro _F_ 1 (InKB) on the in-domain test set and four out-of-domain test sets for the entity linking task.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text describes seven out-of-domain EL datasets: MSNBC, Derczynski, KORE50, R128, R500, OKE15, and OKE16. The displayed table also appears to include these seven columns in addition to AIDA. The caption’s reference to four out-of-domain test sets is therefore inconsistent with the experimental description and likely a caption error.

comments.title: Reported EL improvement margins do not clearly match the table
comments.quote: The improvements on EL are instead more evident. GENRE is the best in-domain system for AIDA while performing remarkably well also on the out-of-domain setting (e.g., +13 _F_ 1 points on Derczynski, and +4.7 on KORE50).
comments.explanation: The qualitative claim that GENRE performs strongly on EL is supported, but the stated Derczynski margin is difficult to reconcile with the displayed table. GENRE is listed at 54.1, while the best visible non-GENRE Derczynski value appears to be 42.4, implying about +11.7 rather than +13. The KORE50 margin is more plausible. The table formatting may explain the discrepancy, but the reported Derczynski improvement appears imprecise as written.

comments.title: Dense-retrieval comparison is confounded by different training regimes
comments.quote: No model except ours was trained on the entire KILT dataset at the same time. A RAG model was trained for every single task as well as for DPR+BERT. Note that this gives and advantage to RAG and DPR+BERT to specialize on single tasks where we have only a single model to solve all of them which still performs better. We speculate that multi-task training could have helped since the all tasks share a common objective to retrieve entities.
comments.explanation: The authors acknowledge that GENRE and the baselines were trained under different regimes, but the interpretation remains difficult. Task-specific training may benefit RAG and DPR+BERT, while GENRE’s multi-task exposure across KILT may also improve retrieval. Since training data and multi-task transfer can materially affect retrieval performance, the comparison does not isolate architectural or retrieval-method gains from training-regime effects. The main text should more clearly separate these factors.

comments.title: Evidence that GENRE “uses the context more effectively” is suggestive but not conclusive
comments.quote: Different is the case of partial and no match: GENRE performance is much higher suggesting that our model uses the context more effectively, as the autoregressive formulation allows to cross-encode mention context and entity candidates directly capturing fine-grained interactions between the two.
comments.explanation: Higher accuracy on partial- and no-match cases is consistent with GENRE exploiting more than exact mention-title overlap. Still, the causal attribution is stronger than the analysis establishes. The pattern could also reflect title priors, memorization, constraints, or compositional title semantics. In addition, GENRE conditions generated title tokens on context, but it does not cross-encode a separately provided candidate representation in the usual cross-encoder sense. The claim should be framed as a plausible interpretation rather than a demonstrated mechanism.

comments.title: Rarity analysis measures mention-entity pair frequency, not necessarily entity rarity, and the bucket definition is underspecified
comments.quote: The degradation in performance is minimal indicating that our model is good at predicting rare entities. / Figure 3: Accuracy per mention-entity pair frequency (in Wikipedia) on the validation sets of all Entity Disambiguation tasks in KILT.
comments.explanation: The figure directly concerns mention-entity pair frequency, which is not the same as entity frequency. A rare surface-form/entity pairing may involve a frequent entity, and a genuinely rare entity would require aggregation over all its mentions. The caption also does not specify how frequencies are counted or binned, e.g. whether labels denote exact counts or ranges, and what source in Wikipedia is used for the counts. The evidence therefore more directly supports robustness to rare mention-entity pairings than to rare entities.

comments.title: Cold-start evaluation omits the candidate set or negative pool used at test time
comments.quote: We manually collect 50 Wikipedia articles that were created in 2020 to simulate a _cold-start_ setting where new entities are added to the KB and the only entity information available is their names. To create ED instances we resort to hyperlinks pointing to those entities in other Wikipedia articles.
comments.explanation: For constrained-decoding entity disambiguation, the difficulty of the cold-start experiment depends heavily on the candidate set. The paper does not specify whether decoding is performed against the full existing Wikipedia title trie plus the 50 new titles, only the 50 new entities, a mention-specific candidate set, or some other pool. This matters especially because many cases are exact mention-title matches. Without the candidate-pool definition, the evaluation is hard to reproduce and interpret.

comments.title: Seen/unseen comparison remains confounded by possible BART pretraining exposure
comments.quote: Surprisingly, GENRE performs almost the same for seen and unseen entity pairs (64.4 vs 63.2 accuracy) However, in the Onoe & Durrett (2020) setting we cannot guarantee entity descriptions have not been seen by BART during pre-training (given his training data contains Wikipedia).
comments.explanation: The authors correctly note the pretraining caveat, but it substantially weakens the interpretation of the seen-versus-unseen result. If entities or their descriptions appeared in BART’s pretraining data, similar accuracy on unseen fine-tuning pairs does not cleanly demonstrate generalization to truly unseen entity knowledge. The result should be interpreted with this limitation foregrounded.

comments.title: Copying-constraint wording may conflate entity-trie decoding with markup copying constraints
comments.quote: Our constrained beam search encompasses both aspects, a copying mechanism that restrains the vocabulary and a structural constraint to obtain a well-formed annotated output.
comments.explanation: The copying restriction applies to the end-to-end markup setting, where the decoder is constrained to copy source tokens except when emitting mention/entity markup. The main entity-name decoding used for retrieval is instead trie-based over valid entity identifiers and does not require copying entity names from the input. As written, the statement could make readers think GENRE’s general constrained beam search hard-constrains entity generation through copying, rather than using different constraints in different task settings.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is well motivated and clearly written, with a compelling central idea: reframing entity retrieval as constrained autoregressive generation of canonical entity names rather than classification over atomic entity IDs. The introduction does a strong job identifying practical limitations of dense bi-encoder retrieval—memory footprint, negative sampling, limited interaction modeling—and the examples in Figure 1 effectively illustrate why entity names can provide useful compositional structure. The main contribution appears original and significant, especially if the reported broad empirical gains and memory reductions hold across the many claimed tasks. That said, the framing risks overstating some advantages: generating names still requires constrained decoding over a large entity vocabulary/trie, so latency and scalability should be examined as carefully as memory; “exact softmax” is exact over next-token vocabularies rather than over entities, which may introduce length and surface-form biases; and the method’s dependence on unique, stable, unambiguous names may be a limitation for KBs without Wikipedia-like titles or for entities with many aliases. The claim that the model “cross-encodes” context and entity is also somewhat different from cross-encoding candidate descriptions, since candidate-specific evidence is represented only through the generated string unless additional metadata is used. The most important issues for the full paper to address are therefore rigorous efficiency comparisons, robustness to ambiguous/rare/new entities and aliases, calibration/ranking behavior under constrained beam search, and whether gains come from genuine retrieval ability versus memorized factual/name priors in the pretrained sequence-to-sequence model. Overall, the paper appears strong, readable, and potentially impactful, but its practical trade-offs and assumptions about entity naming need careful empirical validation.

comments.title: Memory-scaling claim omits the entity-name index needed for constrained retrieval
comments.quote: (ii) the memory footprint is greatly reduced because the parameters of our encoder-decoder architecture scale with vocabulary size, not entity count;
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: unlike bi-encoder/entity-classifier approaches, GENRE does not need to store one dense vector or one learned output weight per entity, so the learned model parameters are not linearly tied to the number of entities. That is a real advantage, and the later method section confirms that the dense-vector footprint is much larger than the trie used for decoding. However, the wording here ties the overall memory-footprint reduction to parameter scaling, which can mislead a reader into thinking retrieval memory is independent of the entity set size. In fact, constrained decoding requires storing the valid entity identifiers, e.g. as a prefix trie, and the full context later states that the Wikipedia-title trie has millions of leaves/internal nodes and occupies about 600MB. Thus the model parameters may not scale with entity count, but the retrieval apparatus still does. The claim would be more precise if it distinguished learned-parameter memory from candidate-set/index memory.

comments.title: “Exact softmax loss” may be read as an entity-level softmax, but the exact normalization is token-level
comments.quote: (iii) the exact softmax loss can be efficiently computed without the need to subsample negative data.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intended point is clear from the broader method: by formulating retrieval as sequence generation, training uses the standard seq2seq token-level cross-entropy, whose vocabulary softmax can be computed exactly at each decoding step. This does avoid the negative-entity sampling commonly used when training a classifier over millions of entities. What remains potentially misleading is that, in the surrounding discussion, the baseline problem is an exact softmax over entities; the phrase here could therefore be read as saying GENRE computes an exact entity-level softmax over all non-gold entities. It does not: the likelihood is normalized over next-token vocabulary choices, and probability mass is assigned to arbitrary strings as well as entity titles unless constrained at inference. Thus the method avoids entity negative sampling by changing the objective, not by exactly normalizing over all candidate entities.

comments.title: Adding new entities by name is methodologically possible but stated more strongly than the evidence implies
comments.quote: Finally, we demonstrate that new entities can be added by simply specifying their unambiguous name.
comments.explanation: The claim captures an appealing and genuine property of the approach: a new entity title can be inserted into the allowed-output set without learning a new entity embedding or retraining a classifier head. The context also makes clear that GENRE relies only on titles, which supports this design. Still, the wording can overstate what is guaranteed. Adding a name to the candidate set makes the entity decodable, but it does not by itself give the model new factual knowledge, aliases, descriptions, or associations needed to retrieve that entity from arbitrary contexts. Performance for a genuinely novel entity will depend on whether the context contains copyable/compositional cues or patterns already learned by the model. A more careful phrasing would separate ‘can be added to the candidate set’ from ‘can be reliably retrieved.’

comments.title: Autoregressive target is described as a set rather than a sequence
comments.quote: where _[y]_[is][the] set of _N_ tokens in the identifier of _e_
comments.explanation: The intended formulation is clear from the surrounding text: GENRE generates an entity name left-to-right, and the factorization uses positions \(y_i\) and prefixes \(y_{<i}\). That only makes sense if \(y\) is an ordered sequence of tokens. Earlier in Section 2 the authors also define the entity name as “a sequence of tokens _y_,” so the mathematical intent is resolved by context. What remains problematic is the word “set”: a set has no order and no multiplicity, so it is formally inconsistent with the autoregressive factorization. This is likely just imprecise wording rather than a substantive methodological error, but it should be corrected to “sequence” to avoid confusion.

comments.title: Memory-scaling claim ignores candidate-set/trie storage
comments.quote: Also, the memory footprint required is orders of magnitude smaller than current systems, since the parameters of a sequence-to-sequence model scale linearly with the vocabulary size, not entity count.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that the neural model parameters do not require one dense vector per entity, unlike bi-encoder indexes, and this is an important advantage. However, the retrieval system still needs a representation of the valid entity-name set for constrained decoding. Later context confirms that the Wikipedia-title trie has millions of leaves and internal nodes and occupies about 600MB, so some memory still scales with the number and structure of entities. Thus the statement is accurate only if “parameters” are being contrasted with dense entity embeddings, but it is too broad as a statement about the total memory footprint of the retriever. The phrase “orders of magnitude” is also somewhat stronger than what the immediately cited examples suggest unless the comparison includes particular large indexes or precision choices. A more precise claim would distinguish model-parameter memory from candidate-constraint memory.

comments.title: Token-level exact softmax may be confused with entity-level exact softmax
comments.quote: Moreover, the exact softmax can be computed efficiently for each output token (i.e., all non-gold tokens are considered negative), thereby eliminating the need for negative data downsampling.
comments.explanation: The local statement is technically understandable: in a seq2seq objective, the model computes the full vocabulary softmax at each decoder step, so it does not need to sample negative entity labels in the way a classifier over millions of entities would. The authors also qualify this as being “for each output token,” which helps. Still, the preceding discussion contrasts GENRE with exact softmax over all entities, and a reader could infer that GENRE is computing an exact entity-level normalizer over all valid entity names. It is not: the training objective normalizes over next-token vocabulary choices at each prefix, not over the entire entity set, and non-gold entities are only contrasted indirectly through their next differing token. This does not invalidate the method, but the wording slightly blurs the distinction between exact token-level likelihood and exact entity-level classification likelihood.

comments.title: Beam search wording overstates top-k guarantee
comments.quote: Instead of explicitly scoring all entities in _E_ , we search for the top- _k_ entities in _E_ decoding from our model using BS with _k_ beams.
comments.explanation: The authors do acknowledge just before this sentence that beam search is an approximate decoding strategy, so the intended meaning is mostly clear. However, the quoted wording can still be read as saying that beam search with \(k\) beams returns the true top-\(k\) entities under the model score. Standard beam search does not provide that guarantee, especially for variable-length autoregressive outputs and constrained tries; it returns a heuristic set of high-scoring sequences that may omit globally higher-scoring candidates pruned at earlier prefixes. This is a localized phrasing issue rather than a flaw in the method, but “approximate top-\(k\)” or “candidate top-\(k\)” would be more accurate.

comments.title: Trie description omits terminal/EOS handling for entity names that are prefixes of longer names
comments.quote: Thus, we define our constrain in terms of a prefix tree _T_ (aka trie) (Cormen et al., 2009) where nodes are annotated with tokens from the vocabulary. For each node _t ∈T_ , its children indicate all the allowed continuations from the prefix defined traversing the trie from the root to _t_ .
comments.explanation: The intended constrained decoding scheme is clear: maintain the current prefix in a trie of valid entity identifiers and mask out vocabulary tokens that are not valid next steps. That is a standard and appropriate way to ensure generated strings are entity names. What remains underspecified is how sequence termination is represented. In a trie over tokenized titles, some valid identifiers can be prefixes of other valid identifiers, e.g. a title like "Mercury" versus longer disambiguated titles beginning with the same token sequence. If children alone define allowed continuations, a node corresponding to a complete entity name but also having descendants needs an explicit terminal flag or EOS child; otherwise the decoder either cannot stop at that valid entity or may stop at prefixes that are not valid identifiers. A reader can infer that EOS is probably treated as a token or that terminal nodes are marked, but the passage does not state this boundary condition, and it affects exact reproducibility of constrained beam search.

comments.title: Dynamic markup-constrained decoding is described at a high level but leaves key state transitions implicit
comments.quote:
When outside a mention/entity step the decoder has only two options: (i) to continue by copying the next token from the input source, or (ii) to generate the _start of mention_ token (i.e., ‘[’) which makes the decoder enter the mention generating phase. While

generating a mention, the decoder has either to continue with the next token in the input source or to generate the _end of mention_ token (i.e., ‘]’) which makes the decoder enter the entity generating phase. Finally, when generating an entity, the decoder employs the entities trie such that it can only output a valid entity identifier as in Constrained Beam Search explained above.
comments.explanation: The overall idea is sensible: constrain the output to be a monotonic copy of the input with inserted mention and entity markup, and use the entity-name trie only inside the link portion. However, for a core algorithmic contribution, several necessary transition details are left implicit. The decoder must maintain at least an input-position pointer, a markup state, and an entity-trie state; it must decide when EOS is allowed at the end of the source; it must prevent or define behavior for empty mentions; and while generating an entity it must specify when the closing delimiter is allowed, especially when one entity name is a prefix of another. Figure 2’s caption partially suggests a closure rule, but the prose only says the trie ensures a valid identifier, not how closing and continuation are jointly handled. These are likely straightforward implementation choices, but without them the dynamic constraint procedure is not fully reproducible from the description.

comments.title: Calling the predefined entity set “relatively small” may obscure that tractability depends on trie precomputation, not on a small candidate set
comments.quote: Differently from a setting where the output space is relatively small (e.g., a pre-defined set _E_ ), the space of annotated outputs is exponentially large.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: ordinary entity retrieval has a finite, enumerable set of valid outputs, so a trie can be precomputed, whereas end-to-end markup outputs combine many possible span segmentations and entity assignments and therefore cannot be enumerated in the same way. That distinction is valid. The wording "relatively small," however, is potentially misleading in context because earlier the paper emphasizes that _E_ can contain about six million Wikipedia entities. The issue is not really that _E_ is small, but that it is a fixed finite set with substantial shared prefixes and thus a compact trie representation. This is mostly a framing ambiguity rather than a technical error, but a careful reader could otherwise read it as contradicting the large-scale retrieval setting.

comments.title: Table 2 caption understates the number of out-of-domain EL datasets
comments.quote: Table 2: Micro _F_ 1 (InKB) on the in-domain test set and four out-of-domain test sets for the entity linking task.
comments.explanation: I first tried to read this as a harmless caption summary, but the surrounding experimental description says the EL setting evaluates on seven out-of-domain test sets: MSNBC, Derczynski, KORE50, R128, R500, OKE15, and OKE16. The table header in the passage also appears to include those seven out-of-domain columns in addition to AIDA. Thus the caption's “four out-of-domain test sets” is inconsistent with both the methods text and the displayed columns. This is likely just a caption error rather than a methodological flaw, but it could mislead readers about the scope of the EL evaluation.

comments.title: Reported EL improvement margins do not clearly match the table
comments.quote: The improvements on EL are instead more evident. GENRE is the best in-domain system for AIDA while performing remarkably well also on the out-of-domain setting (e.g., +13 _F_ 1 points on Derczynski, and +4.7 on KORE50).
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is well supported: GENRE is strong on AIDA and improves over prior systems on several EL datasets. However, when I compare the stated margins with Table 2 as displayed, the exact numbers are hard to reconcile. GENRE has 54.1 on Derczynski; the best non-GENRE value visible for the Der column appears to be 42.4, which would be about +11.7 rather than +13. For KORE50, GENRE has 60.7, and the closest visible prior best appears around 56.1 or 55.9, giving roughly +4.6 to +4.8, so +4.7 is plausible. Because the OCR table is garbled and the original formatting may clarify which values belong to which columns, I would not treat this as a major error. Still, the Derczynski margin as written seems at least imprecise unless there is an unshown baseline or different rounding convention.

comments.title: DR comparison is potentially confounded by different training regimes
comments.quote: No model except ours was trained on the entire KILT dataset at the same time. A RAG model was trained for every single task as well as for DPR+BERT. Note that this gives and advantage to RAG and DPR+BERT to specialize on single tasks where we have only a single model to solve all of them which still performs better. We speculate that multi-task training could have helped since the all tasks share a common objective to retrieve entities.
comments.explanation: The authors do acknowledge an important caveat: GENRE is trained jointly on multiple KILT datasets, whereas several baselines are task-specific or not trained on KILT in the same way. What remains problematic is the interpretation of this asymmetry. The passage initially frames task-specific training as an advantage for RAG and DPR+BERT, but immediately notes that GENRE's multi-task training may itself have helped. In retrieval and QA benchmarks, differences in training data and multitask exposure can substantially affect performance, so the claim of “superiority” on DR is not fully isolated from the training-regime confound. The later mention of Appendix B controls helps, but in this passage the main comparison would be more careful if it explicitly separated architectural/retrieval-method gains from gains due to broader multitask training.

comments.title: Evidence for “uses the context more effectively” is suggestive but not conclusive
comments.quote: Different is the case of partial and no match: GENRE performance is much higher suggesting that our model uses the context more effectively, as the autoregressive formulation allows to cross-encode mention context and entity candidates directly capturing fine-grained interactions between the two.
comments.explanation: The observed pattern is interesting and relevant: better performance when the mention and entity name only partially overlap, or do not overlap, is consistent with GENRE exploiting more than exact string matching. The authors also strengthen the argument by comparing against a numerical-ID variant. Still, the stated mechanism is somewhat stronger than what the described analysis alone establishes. Higher accuracy on partial/no-overlap cases could arise from several factors besides more effective contextual reasoning, such as entity-name priors, memorization from training, differences in candidate constraints, or the compositional semantics of titles. Also, “cross-encode mention context and entity candidates” is an informal characterization: GENRE conditions generated title tokens on the input context, but it does not score a separately encoded candidate description with full cross-attention in the standard cross-encoder sense. The claim is plausible, but the causal attribution should be presented as suggestive rather than demonstrated.

comments.title: Rare-entity conclusion is stronger than the measured frequency supports
comments.quote: The performance of a model naturally depends on how many times entities appear in the training data. We show the data distribution of the mention-entity frequency in Figure 3. ... The degradation in performance is minimal indicating that our model is good at predicting rare entities.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended analysis: the authors are trying to show that GENRE is not merely memorizing frequent Wikipedia hyperlink pairs, and that performance remains reasonable even in the long tail. The intermediate phrasing correctly says the plotted quantity is the frequency of mention-entity pairs. What remains problematic is the final inference to “rare entities.” A rare mention-entity pair is not necessarily a rare entity: an entity may be very frequent overall but rarely linked with a particular surface form, and conversely an entity-frequency analysis would require aggregating over all mentions of the entity. Thus the evidence supports robustness to rare or unseen mention-entity pairings more directly than robustness to rare entities. This is a localized framing issue, but it could mislead readers about what was actually measured.

comments.title: Cold-start evaluation omits the candidate set / negative pool used at test time
comments.quote: We manually collect 50 Wikipedia articles that were created in 2020[6] to simulate a _cold-start_ setting where new entities are added to the KB and the only entity information available is their names. To create ED instances we resort to hyperlinks pointing to those entities in other Wikipedia articles.
comments.explanation: The setup makes sense in spirit: entities created after the 2019 dumps are a reasonable proxy for newly added KB entries, and hyperlinks to those pages provide naturally occurring mention contexts. However, for an entity-disambiguation experiment with constrained decoding, the difficulty depends crucially on the candidate set. It is not clear whether the model decodes against the full 2019 Wikipedia title trie plus the 50 new titles, only the 50 collected new entities, a mention-specific candidate set, or some other pool. This matters especially because 19/50 cases are exact mention-title matches; their significance is very different under a full-Wikipedia candidate set than under a tiny candidate set. The paper has earlier been careful about candidate sets in ED, so the omission here leaves an important reproducibility and interpretability gap.

comments.title: Cold-start claim about existing systems is overbroad without baselines
comments.quote: This demonstrates the ability of our solution to be applied in scenarios where entity metadata is unavailable (apart his name), a setting where, to the best of our knowledge, no existing system is capable to operate.
comments.explanation: The experiment does demonstrate an attractive property of GENRE: adding a new entity name to the constrained decoding set can make the entity immediately reachable without learning a new classifier label or encoding a description. That said, the stronger claim that no existing system can operate when only the entity name is available is not established here. Lexical title matching, sparse retrieval over titles, alias tables, or dense bi-encoders that encode title-only metadata could all in principle operate in such a setting, even if they might perform worse. Since no title-only baseline is reported for the cold-start setup, the conclusion should be narrowed to GENRE’s demonstrated capability rather than a broad incapability of prior systems.

comments.title: Seen/unseen comparison may be confounded by BART pretraining exposure
comments.quote: Surprisingly, GENRE performs almost the same for seen and unseen entity pairs (64.4 vs 63.2 accuracy) However, in the Onoe & Durrett (2020) setting we cannot guarantee entity descriptions have not been seen by BART during pre-training (given his training data contains Wikipedia).
comments.explanation: The authors appropriately acknowledge a pretraining-exposure caveat, which is important because BART may have absorbed Wikipedia facts or descriptions before GENRE fine-tuning. The remaining issue is that this caveat substantially weakens the interpretation of the “seen vs unseen entity pairs” result: if the underlying entities or descriptions appeared in pretraining, then similar accuracy on unseen fine-tuning pairs does not cleanly show generalization to truly unseen entities or even unseen entity knowledge. The statement is not false, and the caveat is present, but the preceding “surprisingly” framing still risks overstating what can be concluded from this comparison.

comments.title: “Exact softmax” claim is easy to read as entity-level normalization
comments.quote: the ability to compute an exact softmax without the need to subsample negative data
comments.explanation: The intended contrast with classifier-style entity retrieval is clear: GENRE trains with the standard decoder softmax over the token vocabulary at each generation step, so it does not need to sample negative entities as dense-retrieval/classification systems often do. Earlier sections partially resolve this by defining the autoregressive factorization and explaining that the loss is exact at the token level. What remains potentially misleading is that, in an entity-retrieval context, “exact softmax” could naturally be interpreted as an exact softmax over the entity set or candidate set. GENRE does not compute such an entity-level normalizer during training; it changes the objective to sequence likelihood over titles. Moreover, at constrained decoding time the paper states that invalid tokens are masked at the log-probability level without renormalizing, so the constrained scores are not a normalized probability distribution over valid entities either. The claim is therefore substantively right for the decoder vocabulary softmax, but should be qualified to avoid implying an exact entity-level softmax comparable to a multiclass entity classifier.

comments.title: Frequency-bucket definition in Figure 3 is underspecified
comments.quote: Figure 3: Accuracy per mention-entity pair frequency (in Wikipedia) on the validation sets of all Entity Disambiguation tasks in KILT.
comments.explanation: The purpose of the figure is understandable: the authors are analyzing whether GENRE degrades on rare mention–entity pairs, and the log-scaled x-axis labels appear to be powers of two, with a separate “None” bucket. However, the caption does not specify exactly how the frequencies are computed or binned. A careful reader cannot tell whether a label such as \(2^{5}\) denotes exactly 32 appearances, a bin such as [32, 63], or some other logarithmic grouping; nor is it clear from the caption whether “in Wikipedia” means hyperlink anchor counts, BLINK pretraining triples, KILT provenance pages, all article text after string matching, or another dump-specific statistic. This matters because the figure supports a robustness-to-rarity claim, and different counting conventions can materially change which examples are considered rare or unseen. The issue is localized and likely easy to fix with one sentence in the caption or surrounding text.

comments.title: Copying constraint description may conflate two different constrained-decoding mechanisms
comments.quote: Our constrained beam search encompasses both aspects, a copying mechanism that restrains the vocabulary and a structural constraint to obtain a well-formed annotated output.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended comparison to prior constrained generation work: in the end-to-end entity-linking markup setting, the dynamic decoder both forces structural well-formedness and, when outside or inside a mention, restricts generation to either copying the next input token or emitting a markup token. That is a useful combination. The phrasing is nevertheless a bit too broad because the main entity-name constrained beam search used for ED/DR is trie-based over valid entity identifiers, not a copying mechanism from the input. Copying is part of the dynamic markup decoding procedure for EL, while the entity-link portion follows the entity trie. As written, “Our constrained beam search” could make readers think the general GENRE decoding method hard-constrains entity names by copying source tokens, which is not the case. This is mostly a terminology/precision issue, but it could mislead readers about what constraints are actually imposed in the different tasks.
